extractions.problem: Multicellular systems problems require simulating both biochemical microenvironment and many mechanically/biochemically interacting cells, but existing tools lack scalability or accessibility.
extractions.method: Open-source agent-based simulator (PhysiCell) in C++ with multi-substrate biotransport solver, OpenMP parallelization, and built-in sub-models for cell cycling, death, mechanics, and motility.
extractions.finding: PhysiCell achieves linear performance scaling and simulates 10^5-10^6 cells on desktop workstations; demonstrated emergent tissue-scale dynamics in tumor spheroids, DCIS, biorobots, and cancer immunology.
extractions.dataset: Hanging drop tumor spheroids, ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), synthetic biorobots, cancer heterogeneity/immunology models
extractions.metric: Cell count scaling, tumor diameter, computational wall time, linear speedup with threads
extractions.baseline: Other 3-D multicellular platforms (CompuCell3D, Chaste, Biocellion, Timothy)
extractions.result: ~10^6 cells in 18-day HDS simulation, linear scaling with cell number, feasible on quad-core desktop

extractions.problem: Understanding how tumor-induced angiogenesis affects the growth rate, size, and morphology of solid tumors in 3D over long time periods.
extractions.method: Glazier-Graner-Hogeweg (Cellular Potts) multi-cell model coupled with PDE-based oxygen and pro-angiogenic factor fields, simulating tumor cell proliferation, hypoxia, necrosis, and endothelial cell chemotaxis.
extractions.finding: Vascularized tumors grow faster and transition through distinct morphological phases (spherical → cylindrical → paddle-shaped); avascular tumors form cylinders following blood vessels rather than remaining spherical.
extractions.dataset: Simulated 3D vascular tumor with pre-existing vasculature in 850×850×850 µm domain
extractions.metric: Tumor volume, growth rate, morphology (diameter), number of cells over time
extractions.result: Avascular tumor max diameter ~200 µm; vascularized tumor ~300 µm diameter; 75-day simulation

extractions.problem: Several gene-deficiency models produce innate CD8+ T cells with memory phenotype, but the underlying mechanism driving this cell-extrinsic differentiation was unknown.
extractions.method: Mixed bone marrow chimeras, flow cytometry for surface markers and transcription factors, Listeria monocytogenes infection model, and genetic crosses with IL-4Rα-/-, PLZF-/- mice.
extractions.finding: PLZF+ T cells produce IL-4 that drives memory-like CD8+ T cell development; this mechanism operates in KLF2-, ITK-, and CBP-deficient mice and underlies innate CD8+ T cells in BALB/c mice.
extractions.dataset: KLF2-deficient, ITK-deficient, CBP-deficient, and BALB/c mouse models
extractions.metric: Flow cytometry (CD44, CXCR3, Eomes, T-bet expression), IFN-γ intracellular staining, OT-I competitive transfer ratios
extractions.baseline: Wild-type bone marrow chimeras, all-WT thymic environment
extractions.result: Bystander CD8+ T cells show elevated CD44, CD122, Eomes, rapid IFN-γ; IL-4Rα/PLZF double-deficiency abolishes phenotype

extractions.problem: As biological network models grow large and revisions frequent, it becomes difficult to manage equations directly or combine previously developed models; no existing approach fully meets transparency, extensibility, and reusability needs.
extractions.method: PySB framework embedding rule-based modeling (BioNetGen/Kappa) within Python, using macros for biochemical actions (bind, catalyze, assemble) and ODE integration via scipy/VODE.
extractions.finding: PySB enables modular, reusable, and version-controlled biological models as full Python programs; demonstrated by generating 18 EARM apoptosis model variants from shared reusable components.
extractions.dataset: Extrinsic apoptosis reaction models (EARM 2.0), apoptosis signaling networks
extractions.metric: Model expressiveness, code reusability, number of variant models generated
extractions.result: 18 apoptosis model variants from reusable modules; models publicly available on GitHub

extractions.problem: Drug development suffers high attrition rates due to poor pharmacokinetic and safety properties; rapid computational ADMET prediction is needed early in the pipeline.
extractions.method: pkCSM uses distance-based graph signatures derived from molecular graphs combined with machine learning to build 30 ADMET predictors (14 regression + 16 classification) across absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity.
extractions.finding: pkCSM achieves performance as good as or better than existing methods, with significant improvement on 11 datasets; scalable to large screening datasets (>10,000 compounds).
extractions.dataset: Multiple ADMET datasets: up to 18,000 compounds (metabolism), 10,000+ (rat toxicity), Caco2, T. pyriformis, minnow toxicity
extractions.baseline: Existing ADMET prediction methods (various published benchmarks)
extractions.result: Significant improvement on 11 datasets (water solubility, Caco2, rat/T. pyriformis/minnow toxicity, P-gp, CYP450 1A2/C19/2C9)

extractions.problem: Predicting toxicity of therapeutic peptides/proteins before synthesis is critical for peptide-based drug discovery, but no prior method used experimentally validated non-toxic peptides.
extractions.method: SVM and KNN models using amino acid composition, dipeptide composition, and AAP (amino acid pair) profiles, trained on experimentally validated toxic and non-toxic peptides from multiple databases.
extractions.finding: Achieved 54-86% accuracy depending on features; dipeptide composition was the best discriminator; first method using experimentally validated non-B-cell epitopes as negative examples.
extractions.dataset: 1805 toxic peptides (ATDB, ArachnoServer, ConoServer, DBETH, SwissProt) + 3893 non-toxic (SwissProt) / 13541 non-toxic (TrEMBL)
extractions.baseline: Random peptides as non-toxic (previous methods like BTXpred, NTXpred)
extractions.result: Dipeptide SVM: accuracy 81.24%, MCC=0.61, AUC=0.88; confirmed dataset accuracy up to 86%

extractions.problem: Predicting MHC class II peptide binding affinity and identifying the peptide binding core is critical for understanding adaptive immune responses but existing methods have limited accuracy.
extractions.method: NN-align: artificial neural network trained with novel algorithm correcting binding core redundancy bias, incorporating peptide flanking residue information for simultaneous core identification and affinity prediction.
extractions.finding: NN-align consistently outperforms TEPITOPE, SMM-align, and NetMHCIIpan across six independent benchmark datasets covering 14 HLA-DR alleles; flanking residues significantly improve accuracy.
extractions.dataset: IEDB HLA-DR benchmark (14 alleles, 14,000+ peptide binding values), SYFPEITHI ligands, Lin et al. and Wang et al. benchmarks
extractions.result: Average AUC=0.798 (NN-W) vs TEPITOPE 0.718, SMM-align 0.778; improved on 12/14 alleles

extractions.problem: Existing methods for predicting linear B-cell epitopes use random peptides as negatives and small datasets, leading to unreliable performance; improved methods with experimentally validated data are needed.
extractions.method: SVM and KNN models using binary profiles, dipeptide composition, and AAP profiles, trained on experimentally validated B-cell epitopes and non-epitopes from IEDB database.
extractions.finding: First method using experimentally validated non-B-cell epitopes; achieved 54-86% accuracy; dipeptide composition is the most discriminative feature; models perform differently on validated vs random negatives.
extractions.dataset: Lbtope_Fixed (12063 epitopes, 20589 non-epitopes), Lbtope_Variable (14876 epitopes, 23321 non-epitopes), Lbtope_Confirm (1042/1795)
extractions.baseline: ABCpred, BepiPred, BCPred (using random peptide negatives)
extractions.result: Dipeptide SVM on Lbtope_Fixed: accuracy 81.24%, MCC=0.61, AUC=0.88

extractions.problem: Predicting conformational B-cell epitopes currently requires 3D antigen structure; no method exists to predict them from amino acid sequence alone.
extractions.method: SVM models using composition profile of patterns (CPP) — encoding amino acid composition within sliding windows — trained on 187 non-redundant antigen chains from IEDB and benchmark datasets.
extractions.finding: Sequence-based CPP model achieves performance comparable to structure-based methods (DiscoTope, CEP, PEPITO), demonstrating conformational B-cell epitope prediction is feasible from primary sequence.
extractions.dataset: 187 non-redundant protein chains (2261 antibody-interacting residues), benchmark 52 antigens
extractions.result: CPP model: MCC=0.73, accuracy=86.59%; comparable to structure-based DiscoTope (MCC=0.20) and CEP (MCC=0.10)

extractions.problem: Genome-scale metabolic models predict maximal biomass yield but not actual growth rate, because they rely only on stoichiometry without enzyme kinetic considerations.
extractions.method: MOMENT (MetabOlic Modeling with ENzyme kineTics) integrates enzyme turnover rates and molecular weights into constraint-based metabolic modeling, accounting for isozymes, protein complexes, and a physiological bound on total enzyme concentration.
extractions.finding: Enzymes catalyzing high-flux reactions have higher turnover numbers (design principle); MOMENT predicts E. coli growth rates correlated with experimental measurements across diverse media, improving upon FBA.
extractions.dataset: E. coli K-12 BW25113 grown on diverse carbon sources in M9 minimal medium
extractions.metric: Pearson correlation (predicted vs measured growth rate and intracellular fluxes)
extractions.result: Pearson r=0.45-0.46 (p<0.001) between turnover rates and fluxes; significant growth rate prediction improvement over FBA
